title: Requirements & Scope Management
summary: Define the right requirements, turn them into scope, and protect value through traceability, validation, and change control.
intro: Turn value into clear requirements, then define and control the work - in baseline or backlog language.

sections.heading: Where Requirements and Scope Live
sections.body: Requirements work supports the Scope and Stakeholders performance domains and the Focus on Value principle: build the right thing, then build it right. Scope is one of the PMBOK 8 performance domains, delivered through its six processes. Read the question for the delivery mode first, then answer in that mode's language.

blocks.title: How scope maps
blocks.text: Scope domain -> organise the work deliberately through requirements, scope statement, WBS or backlog. Scope domain -> deliverables meet scope and quality; benefits are realised. Tailoring -> predictive fixes scope up front; agile lets scope emerge through the backlog.

groups.label: Signals
lines: Stable, well-understood scope.
Scope statement, WBS, WBS dictionary.
Change control board.

groups.label: Handle it by
lines: Lock the scope baseline.
Route any change through integrated change control.
Validate and control against the baseline.

groups.label: Signals
lines: Changing or emerging requirements.
Product backlog, user stories.
Product Owner, sprint reviews.

groups.label: Handle it by
lines: Keep a prioritised, refined backlog.
Let the Product Owner own priority and value.
Accept scope change as normal, not as a threat.

sections.heading: BA vs PM
sections.body: Business analysis is about understanding the problem: defining and managing requirements and the solution scope so the right product gets built. The project manager focuses on delivering the project: planning, coordinating work and managing constraints so it gets built right. On many projects there is no separate business analyst, so the PM performs the BA work; where both roles exist they collaborate closely from problem framing to validating the delivered solution.

groups.label: Focus
lines: Understand the problem and need.
Define and manage requirements.
Own the solution scope.

groups.label: Question
lines: Are we building the right product?

groups.label: Focus
lines: Plan and coordinate the work.
Manage constraints and delivery.
Enable the team to deliver value.

groups.label: Question
lines: Are we building the product right?

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: BA = build the right thing; PM = build the thing right. No separate BA -> the PM does the BA work. PMBOK 8 lens: requirements clarity feeds the Focus on Value principle and the Scope and Stakeholders domains.

sections.heading: Product Scope vs Project Scope
sections.body: Product scope is the set of features and functions that characterise the deliverable: the thing the customer ends up with. Project scope is the work required to produce that deliverable, including the management activities needed to deliver it. Measuring completeness is the giveaway. A question that talks about features satisfied is pointing at product scope; one that talks about work completed is pointing at project scope.

rows.value: Features and functions of the deliverable. Measured against requirements.
Work required to deliver it, plus management activities. Measured against the scope baseline.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: Features delivered = product scope; work performed = project scope.

sections.heading: Requirement Types
sections.body: Requirements sit in a hierarchy from why to what to how-we-transition. Solution requirements split further into functional and non-functional requirements.

rows.value: The why - higher-level goals, objectives and the need the project addresses.
The needs of specific stakeholders or groups; bridges business goals and the solution.
What the product must do and how well it must do it. Splits into functional and non-functional.
Temporary capabilities to move from current to future state - training, data migration, parallel running. Retire once transition is complete.
What the system does - a feature, action, data or behaviour. Example: the system shall let a user reset a password.
How well it performs - performance, security, usability, reliability, scalability. Example: a page must load in under two seconds; data must be encrypted at rest.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: Transition requirements are temporary - they retire once the new state is live. If it answers how fast, how secure or how usable, it is non-functional. Same feature, different lens: functional = what it does; non-functional = how well it does it.

sections.heading: Visioning and Elicitation
sections.body: Set a shared target, measure the gap, then draw out needs from stakeholders and existing material. Choose elicitation techniques by stakeholder availability and the level of uncertainty, not personal habit.

rows.value: A shared, concise picture of the desired future state; keeps decisions aligned.
Strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, threats - frames internal and external factors.
Compares current state with desired state to define the work needed to close the difference.
Targeted, one-to-one or small group; good for detail and sensitive topics.
Bring stakeholders together to build shared understanding and resolve issues quickly.
Generate many ideas or options before narrowing.
See the real process; surfaces tacit needs people forget to mention.
Mine existing material - policies, contracts, current-system docs.
Reach many dispersed stakeholders cheaply.
Make requirements tangible to refine them through feedback.

blocks.title: Choosing a technique
blocks.text: High uncertainty -> workshops and prototyping. Dispersed stakeholders -> surveys and questionnaires. Sensitive or detailed topics -> interviews. Vision sets the target; gap analysis measures the distance to it.

sections.heading: From Requirements to Scope Baseline
sections.body: Analysis turns raw input into clear, agreed requirements, then traces and controls them through delivery. Predictive projects walk the scope process sequence; agile projects fold the front half into ongoing backlog refinement, but the intent is identical: agree what is being built, then confirm it was built.

items.title: Collect requirements
lines: Turn stakeholder needs into documented, agreed, testable statements.
Elicit via interviews, workshops and prototyping.
Agile: capture as backlog items / user stories.

items.title: Analyse, model and prioritise
lines: Build process, data and interface models.
Clarify, deduplicate and resolve conflicts.
Use MoSCoW and order by value and urgency.

items.title: Define scope
lines: Write the project scope statement.
State what is in scope and, crucially, what is out.

items.title: Create WBS
lines: Decompose deliverables into manageable work packages.
Scope baseline = scope statement + WBS + WBS dictionary.

items.title: Trace and baseline
lines: Record each requirement in the RTM.
Link back to source and forward to deliverable and test.
Agree and lock the requirement set as the reference for change.

items.title: Validate and control
lines: Customer or sponsor formally accepts completed deliverables.
Monitor scope status and manage changes to the baseline.
Validate Scope and Control Scope run in parallel but answer different questions.

blocks.title: Validate vs Control
blocks.text: Validate = is it accepted? Customer sign-off. Control = is scope still on baseline? Managing change. Once baselined, changes go through integrated change control, not ad hoc edits.

sections.heading: Traceability and Key Artefacts
sections.body: The requirements traceability matrix links every requirement back to its origin and forward to the deliverable, test and acceptance criterion that satisfy it. This keeps scope honest: nothing is built that no one asked for, and nothing requested is quietly dropped. The scope baseline is the approved yardstick for later decisions and can only change through formal change control.

items.text: Work Breakdown Structure: deliverable-oriented decomposition of the project into work packages.
Detail behind each WBS element: scope, deliverables, acceptance criteria.
Requirements Traceability Matrix: links each requirement to its origin, deliverable, test and acceptance criterion.
Approved scope statement + WBS + WBS dictionary, changeable only by change control.
Statement of Work - narrative description of products or services to be delivered.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: No requirement without a source and a destination - that is what the RTM enforces. If a requirement cannot be tested or traced, it is not yet a requirement.

sections.heading: Acceptance Criteria vs Definition of Done
sections.body: Both say when work is good enough, but at different levels and owned by different people. Both support the Embed Quality principle and the Scope performance domain.

groups.label: What
lines: Specific, testable conditions of acceptance.
Say when a single requirement or story is satisfied.

groups.label: Scope and owner
lines: Story level; varies item to item.
Usually owned by the product owner.

groups.label: What
lines: Fixed checklist of what shippable means.
For example coded, tested, reviewed, documented.

groups.label: Scope and owner
lines: Product level; applies to every item.
Team-owned; does not change story by story.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: Acceptance criteria are per-story and owner-set; DoD is one shared bar for all work. Acceptance criteria vary; the DoD stays constant.

sections.heading: User Stories and INVEST
sections.body: In adaptive work, requirements are often captured as user stories: short statements of who needs something, what they need, and why it matters. The story is not the full requirement package; it is a placeholder for collaboration, clarification, and later confirmation. INVEST is a quality check for user stories and other backlog items, helping the team spot work that is too dependent, vague, oversized, or hard to validate.
sections.tip: A user story should invite discussion. If it reads like a fixed technical specification with no room for conversation, it is probably too rigid for agile use.

groups.label: What
lines: A brief written reminder of the need or feature.

groups.label: Use
lines: Keeps the backlog visible without pretending all details are known upfront.

groups.label: What
lines: Ongoing discussion among the product owner, team, and stakeholders.

groups.label: Use
lines: Turns a short story into shared understanding before and during delivery.

groups.label: What
lines: The evidence that the story meets the intended outcome.

groups.label: Use
lines: Usually expressed through acceptance criteria and acceptance tests.

items.text: Independent enough to plan and deliver without unnecessary coupling.
Negotiable, meaning the detail can evolve through collaboration.
Valuable to a customer, user, or business stakeholder.
Estimable because enough is known to size the work responsibly.
Small enough to complete within a short planning horizon.
Testable through clear conditions or observable results.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: If a story is valuable but too large, do not discard it. Treat it as an epic or split it into smaller slices that still deliver value.

sections.heading: Epics, Personas and Story Maps
sections.body: An epic is a large body of work that is not ready for near-term execution as a single backlog item. Splitting should preserve value, creating smaller pieces that can be developed, tested, reviewed, and demonstrated. Personas and scenarios make requirements less abstract, while story mapping arranges work around the user's journey and helps sequence usable increments.

items.title: Find the user outcome
lines: Clarify the persona, goal, and business reason behind the epic.

items.title: Slice by path or rule
lines: Break work by workflow step, business rule, data variation, channel, or risk area.

items.title: Keep each slice testable
lines: Each resulting story should have acceptance criteria and a visible result.

items.title: Sequence by value and risk
lines: Prioritize slices that validate assumptions, unlock dependencies, or deliver high stakeholder value.

groups.label: What
lines: A representative user profile with needs, goals, constraints, and behaviors.

groups.label: Use
lines: Guides feature choices and reduces the risk of designing for an average user who does not exist.

groups.label: What
lines: A narrative of how a persona performs a task under specific conditions.

groups.label: Use
lines: Surfaces preconditions, exceptions, decisions, and desired results.

groups.label: What
lines: A visual model of user activities, stories, gaps, and release slices.

groups.label: Use
lines: Helps sequence work around usable increments instead of isolated tasks.

sections.heading: Backlog Refinement and Prioritization
sections.body: The product backlog is a living, ordered list of potential work. Refinement keeps it usable by adding detail, removing stale items, improving estimates, clarifying dependencies, and reordering items as value, risk, and stakeholder needs change. In PMBOK terms, refinement supports progressive elaboration and continuous value delivery. The product owner usually owns backlog ordering, but the team contributes technical insight, estimates, risks, and implementation constraints.
sections.tip: Backlog refinement prepares options for planning; it is not the same event as sprint or iteration planning.

rows.value: Clarify the user need, assumptions, acceptance criteria, or business rule.
Use relative sizing such as story points when time-based precision would be misleading.
Move items based on value, urgency, risk reduction, dependencies, or feedback.
Drop items that no longer support the product goal or current business case.
Must have / Should have / Could have / Won't have this time.
Rank requirements when scope must flex against time or budget.
Backlog ordering and refinement.

blocks.title: Priority cue
blocks.text: Agreed priorities steer what gets built first and what is dropped under pressure.

sections.heading: Change, Scope Creep and Gold Plating
sections.body: In predictive work a baseline change is never an in-place edit; it is a request. In agile work, change is absorbed through re-prioritisation of the backlog rather than a change request. Scope creep and gold plating are both unrequested work that consumes budget and schedule, adds risk and can void acceptance. They are problems, not favours.

groups.label: What it is
lines: Scope growing in an uncontrolled way.
Changes slip in without analysis or approval.

groups.label: Pattern
lines: The steady accretion of just one more small thing.

groups.label: What it is
lines: The delivery team adds extras the customer never requested.

groups.label: Pattern
lines: Showing off or using up slack.
Still consumes budget and adds risk.

blocks.title: Baseline change cue
blocks.text: Scope baseline needs changing -> raise a change request. Do not edit the baseline directly. Assess and authorise before work begins.

blocks.title: Same fix for both
blocks.text: Creep is stakeholder-driven, gold plating is team-driven. Both are wrong; both go through change control.

sections.heading: Key Acronyms
sections.body: Use the acronym expansion to find the exam logic when answer choices feel similar.

items.text: Requirements Traceability Matrix - links each requirement to its source, deliverable and test.
Statement of Work - narrative description of products or services to be delivered.
Must, Should, Could, Won't - prioritisation scheme.
Definition of Done - shared checklist for shippable work.
Acceptance Criteria - per-requirement conditions of acceptance.
Non-Functional Requirement - a quality or constraint on behaviour.
Minimum Viable Product - smallest release that delivers and tests value.
Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats.
Work Breakdown Structure - deliverable-oriented decomposition of the project into work packages.

blocks.title: Acronym tip
blocks.text: When you blank, expand the acronym first - the words usually point to the answer.

source: PM Cram — aligned to the current PMBOK Guide and PMI Examination Content Outline.
